title: Introduction to System Design
description: This is an introductory course for beginners who want to learn system design. The course has been divided into two sessions, with each session focusing on specific aspects of system design.
learningObjectives: Understand basics of databases and caching for performance
Learn about scaling, server states, and architecture models

finalProjectDetails.overview: For the final project, apply the knowledge gained throughout the course to the project you are currently working on. Explain why the architecture was chosen, evaluate its pros and cons, and describe how it would scale. Your approach should demonstrate an understanding of databases, caching, scalability, appropriate server state management, and architectural models. You may also explore additional relevant topics that were not covered in class.
planningResources: Various design patterns and principles books

assignment.title: Implementing Caching Strategies and Database Indexes
assignment.objective: Learn to implement basic caching strategies and create indexes to optimize database performance.
assignment.submissionInstructions: Submit screenshots showing your Redis setup and results of indexed query performance testing.

tasks.title: Examine Cache Effectiveness
tasks.description: Access cached data and observe the performance difference.

tasks.title: Query Indexed Database
tasks.description: Run a query on an indexed and non-indexed database table. Compare the performance.

steps.title: Install Redis
steps.description: Install Redis server to use as a caching layer.

steps.title: Setup a Basic Cache with Redis
steps.description: Implement a basic cache for storing key-value pairs using Redis.

steps.title: Create Index on Database
steps.description: Create an index on a sample dataset to improve query performance.

checkForUnderstanding.question: How does caching help in system design?
What is the trade-off of using indexing in databases?

subsections.title: Introduction to Databases
subsections.content: Databases are structured collections of data, used to store and manipulate information. They are essential for designing systems that handle large volumes of data efficiently. Databases allow for data retrieval, modification, and deletion using structured query language (SQL). They support ACID properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability, ensuring reliable transactions.

quizzes.question: What does ACID stand for in databases?

subsections.title: Types of Databases
subsections.content: Databases can be broadly categorized into relational databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, MySQL) and non-relational databases (e.g., MongoDB, Cassandra). Relational databases use tables to store data and SQL for queries. Non-relational databases, also known as NoSQL databases, store data in various formats like documents, key-value pairs, and graphs.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a NoSQL database?

subsections.title: Introduction to Caching
subsections.content: Caching is a technique used to store copies of frequently accessed data in a high-speed memory layer so that future requests can be served faster. It helps improve performance and scalability. Common caching strategies include in-memory caching (e.g., Redis, Memcached) and browser caching.

quizzes.question: What is the primary purpose of caching?

subsections.title: Database Indexing for Performance
subsections.content: Indexing is a technique used in databases to speed up data retrieval operations. It creates pointers to data in tables, significantly reducing the time taken for query responses. While indexing speeds up reads, it can slow down writes as indexes need updating.

quizzes.question: What is the main advantage of indexing in databases?

assignment.title: Design a Scalable, Load-Balanced System
assignment.objective: Apply your understanding of scalability and system architectures to design an efficient scalable system with load balancing.

tasks.title: Create a System Architecture Diagram
tasks.description: Include horizontal scaling and load balancing elements.

tasks.title: Write a Configuration for Load Balancer
tasks.description: Configure an example load balancer setup with annotations.

steps.title: Draw the Architecture Diagram
steps.description: Draw a diagram illustrating the components and data flow for a scalable system using load balancers and microservices.

steps.title: Configure a Sample Load Balancer
steps.description: Set up a simple NGINX or HAProxy configuration to demonstrate load distribution.

checkForUnderstanding.question: What is a key benefit of microservices architecture?
How does horizontal scaling improve system performance?
options: By adding resources to one server

subsections.title: Understanding System Scalability
subsections.content:
Scalability is the capability of a system to handle a growing amount of work or its potential to accommodate growth. There are two main types of scalability: vertical and horizontal scaling. 

- **Vertical Scaling** involves adding resources to a single node (e.g., upgrading CPU, RAM). This approach is often easier to implement but may face physical limits.

- **Horizontal Scaling** involves adding more nodes to the system (e.g., adding more servers to handle load). It is more complex but allows for handling larger loads and redundancy.

quizzes.question: What is the advantage of horizontal scaling?

subsections.title: Introduction to Load Balancers
subsections.content:
Load balancers distribute incoming network traffic across several servers to ensure no single server becomes overwhelmed. 

- **Types of Load Balancers**: 
  - *Hardware Load Balancers*: Physical devices appliance setups.
  - *Software Load Balancers*: Applications such as HAProxy, NGINX.

- **Benefits**:
  - Increases reliability and availability by distributing traffic.
  - Supports horizontal scaling by evenly distributing loads.

quizzes.question: What is the purpose of a load balancer?
Which of these is a software load balancer?

subsections.title: Microservices Architecture
subsections.content:
Microservices architecture is a design pattern in which a single application is composed of many loosely coupled and independently deployable services. 

- **Characteristics**:
  - Encapsulation of business logic within services.
  - Each microservice is focused on a single function or small area.

- **Pros and Cons**:
  - Facilitates easy deployment and scaling.
  - Can introduce challenges with data consistency and management across services.

- **Examples**:
  - Building a retail application with separate services for product catalog, shopping cart, inventory, etc.

quizzes.question: What is the main advantage of microservices?
Which architecture model allows each service to focus on a single area?
